title: Stripping and refinishing old wood furniture
subtitle: Choosing the right stripper for the existing finish, preparing the surface, and selecting a durable new finish
excerpt: A solid wood chair, table, or chest of drawers that looks shabby after years of use can be refinished to last another generation. The process is not complex but requires identifying the existing finish before choosing a stripper, and preparing the surface correctly before applying a new one. This guide covers the diagnosis, the stripping methods, and the finish choices for different types of furniture use.
sourceNotes: Historic England guidance on paint analysis and removal. Traditional Paints Network guidance on furniture finishes. Which? guide to furniture restoration. Bob Flexner, Understanding Wood Finishing (Popular Woodworking Books).

glossaryTerms.definition: A wood finish that cures to a hard, continuous film on the wood surface: varnish, lacquer, shellac, and most paint. Film finishes can be stripped with the correct solvent or chemical stripper. They need full stripping before re-coating with a different finish type.

A finish that soaks into the wood fibres rather than forming a film: raw linseed oil, Danish oil, tung oil, and hard wax oil. Penetrating finishes cannot be stripped; they can only be sanded back. They give the wood a natural feel and are easy to maintain with additional coats.

content.text: The first step is identifying the existing finish, because the stripping method depends on it. A
can be stripped chemically or by heat. A

Methylated spirit test: dab a small amount on an inconspicuous area and rub with a cloth. If the finish dissolves: shellac. If it softens but does not dissolve: lacquer. If it is unaffected: polyurethane varnish, hard wax oil, or oil.
Acetone test (nail polish remover, no acetate): dissolves nitrocellulose lacquer rapidly. Does not affect polyurethane or hard wax oil.
If sandpaper clogs immediately with a gummy resin: an oil finish is present. Sand the surface lightly to give key before adding a new oil coat.

Water-based chemical stripper: safer to use than solvent-based; slower acting. Apply generously, cover with cling film to prevent evaporation, leave 2 to 4 hours. Scrape off with a plastic scraper. Works on most film finishes.
Solvent-based stripper (methylene chloride free): faster, sharper smell. Use outdoors or with good ventilation. Works on thick polyurethane finishes where water-based is slow.
Sanding: for thin finishes or oil finishes. Start with 80 grit, progress to 120, then 180. Sand with the grain. Remove all dust before applying new finish.
Danish or hard wax oil: natural look, easy to apply, maintains with additional coats. Not waterproof; not suitable for table surfaces with hot dishes.
Polyurethane varnish: highly durable, waterproof, resistant to heat and abrasion. The default for tables, chairs, and high-use furniture. Available in matte, satin, and gloss.
Milk paint or chalk paint: for decorative, non-structural furniture. Usually needs a wax or varnish top coat for durability.

attrs.symptom: New varnish is bubbling after application

content.text: Bubbles are caused by applying varnish too quickly in direct sunlight or over warm wood, or by shaking the tin rather than stirring. Sand back lightly to 240 grit, wipe with a tack cloth, and apply the next coat slowly with a quality brush, working with the grain.
